item.title: Staff Post - Rewrite Captain
item.content:encoded: Permission granted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm requesting permission to edit this one from site admins. I am not the original author, though I am the author of the complete rewrite of the idea into its current form. I'd like to make several scientific and tone edits as well as possibly some additions for narrative or added interest.

Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a great idea, but you'd think the foundation would be smart enough to make a dyson sphere around it and use it in a power plant given it's still generating a whopping 3.828×10^14 Watts of energy. I'd absolutely hate to see that go to waste by warming rocks.

item.title: I approve
item.content:encoded: I love this, the thought of the tiny star gives me warm fuzzy feelings. I like the attention to scientific detail and especially the footnote about its predicted fate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the science, particulary the three-year-old and poorly informed Dr. Angel post:

As has been repeatedly pointed out: anomalous. The fact that it violates the laws of physics in measurable ways is the point.

On the polymer, welcome to the modern age of metamaterials and smart matter. Negative refraction, thermoelectric plastics, artificial muscles, flexible glass… in terms of material properties, there ain't much that's impossible these days.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- Firstly can we delete that contingency in the containment procedures? It seems to be pissing just about everybody off, myself included. It's not like it actually adds anything to the article, it really is utterly pointless, and as DrDizzy pointed out, it's not very helpful if low-level personnel need to know how to deal it.

- I also agree with Abettik on how it could use more story – either making it a super-powerful reactor or weaponising it.

- And my perusal of comments has also highlighted a somewhat alarming number of scientific/mathematical errors (see Dr Angels' comment), so… There's that, I guess.

Anyway, it's a nice idea, but the execution is pretty bloody shoddy, IMO. -1

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: That's actually a pretty cool <g> idea. Radioisotope Thermal Generators work on the same principle, and a Sterling Engine would be even more efficient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"In the event a sudden shift in color and/or temperature occurs mimicking the final stages of a star's lifespan, initiate procedure holycrap-04, clearance 04/037 required."

It could be done with more physics and specifics, but the point is you don't need to put the procedure IN the main document unless it is critical to its story. A handwave to supernova/collapse and an external solution still leaves the reader wondering what we have planned. Increases curiosity and tension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, because it isn't that complex, but has all the necessary points to capture my attention, and create a image in my head. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would've upvoted. Redaction in containment procedures is, however, one of my instant-downvote triggers.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the contingency of a [ACCESS DENIED].

That didn't kill it for me yet, but when I saw there was no justification for the redaction, I went to downvote. Jesus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is an old comment and that the text has been edited to say "ACCESS DENIED," but pretty much this. I wouldn't have downvoted if it weren't for censorship in the Containment Procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the core concept, but as it is described the containment proceedures described would have little or no effect. SCPs can be impossible, that's the point, but containment must be achievable and believable. That is also the point. No vote yet. I hope it gets fixed. All I think it really needs is to be scaled back to the point where a planet would be big enough to contain it, barely. That seems like the original intent; it's just described as too powerful. And polymers are not what you make heat sinks out of.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couldn't they send it through a mirror with SCP-093 and leave it there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“SCP-037 is magnetically contained at Site-32. It is housed in a small subterranean room composed of a heat conducting, radiation-resistant polymer”

Too bad polymers SUCK at radiation resistance and head conduction. They are commonly used as thermal insulators and degrade quickly in the presence of intense heat, light and UV radiation.

Even considering that the foundation has access to nano engineered polymers (http://web.mit.edu/nanoengineering/research/polymers.shtml) they are subpar even to aluminum and are ridiculously outperformed by copper heat pipes or even monocrystalline diamond (considering the foundation acquisitive power).

“and evacuated of any atmosphere.”

“SCP-037 is apparently a star approximately 5 cm (2 in) in diameter, with a luminosity of about 1*10-12 times that of our sun and a surface temperature of about 5000 K “

“Spectral analysis and comparison to known celestial bodies suggest that it is a typical (other than its uncharacteristic size) star quickly undergoing the transition to a red giant, though it is unknown if established theories of star formation and aging apply.”

“If standard stellar models hold up”

I always thought the foundation had at least some personnel who know their way into basic physics and astrophysics. It is obvious any established theories on stars don’t apply to this specimen.
Considering:

a) analysis shows that it at least mimics a typical star;

b) its luminosity (3.846×10^14 W) and size (5cm diameter);

c) its mean density to be approximately the same as the Sun’s (1.408×10^3 kg/m3);

It would weight about 92 grams and have a life spam of about 21 seconds.

Also, if the room is evacuated, the only heat transfer mechanism is radiation. The luminosity of a black body this tiny and at 5000K would be something around 2,78*10^5 W (accordingly to Stefan-Boltzmann Law). It would need an effective temperature of 5,45*10^8 K.

Any serious scientist evaluating this object would say at least something about these astounding discrepancies, instead of a mere “we are not sure theories apply”.
“In the contingency of a [ACCESS DENIED].”

Nope, just… nope.

“Containment and transport of SCP-037 have been achieved by the use of powerful electromagnets, to which the artifact aligns itself according to its own magnetic field. The primary challenge to containment thus far has been its powerful electromagnetic emissions, which are intense enough to be easily seen by the naked eye from high Earth orbit. “

So we contain something that has electromagnetic emissions this powerful with a bunch of magnets and hope everything goes as planned? How do we even react when emissions occur? What about all electronic devices that would be rendered useless instantaneously, along with the electromagnets? This containment, along with the entire facility housing this SCP wouldn’t withstand ONE emission.

I’m down voting this, and if I could I would down vote it again. I like the concept though, but the execution is terrible IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't like it. I think it needs some more story. I have two ideas for someone to use:

1) Make this a truly contained, useful SCP. Have the star being used to provide electricity using a water-cooled steam system (like a nuclear reactor) that provides power to [x] number of Foundation sites. Have it magnetically contained and have the reactor large enough to contain it when it does eventually turn into a red giant. Additionally, have the whole reactor core be part of a large electromagnetic launching mechanism (think a giant railgun) that is powered by the star itself and is capable of launching the star into space past the orbit of Jupiter in a few weeks if something unexpected happens. This idea isn't creepy or eerie, but it does help show the enormous resources and capabilities and ingenuity of the Foundation. Think of it as a background article for the other articles, "They have their own star as a power source!"

2) Make it much creepier. Instead of hinting that it will turn into a (little) red giant, say that studies have shown that it might turn into a black hole that could consume the planet. Maybe even hint that its a long-term 'bomb' from some alien civilization. You could mention something about it being obviously artificial. Maybe there is even a way to turn it off that only advanced civilizations would be able to figure it, and its a 'test' for humanity. I don't know, just brainstorming here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The problem with putting [ACCESS DENIED] in the Special Containment Procedures is that they're the Containment Procedures. Imagine, one day a D-class on night duty notices that this star has gone an alarming shade of red. They rush to the Containment Procedures to see what they're supposed to do. They know there's probably not a button you press or anything, but surely there's an important researcher to alert. And then, bam, ACCESS DENIED.

This is the opposite of helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, that's only better in the sense that being repeatedly smashed in the face with a ballpeen hammer is better than being repeatedly smashed in the face with a ballpeen hammer while also being set on fire. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [ACCESS DENIED]? I know what the author wanted to do, but that's pretty exotic nowadays :) Never seen that one before on scp wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The issue I have always had with this is, and as follows-
We have this massively powerful generation of (continuous, fairly stable) energy underground and we aren't using this to power any of our bases? The way I view the Foundation is generally 'scientists are going to try to one-up each other trying to show off using different SCPs in research'. I know it's a minor nitpic, but it's really what breaks it for me, oddly enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't checked on my stuff for *ahem* quite a while, but I thought I'd give this a whirl and see if I can't fix it up.

Magnetically containing things and not stating a field strength is becoming a pet-peeve of mine.
I personally think that such specificity is over-complicated and risks losing the reader, and is in any case more technical information than is needed in a short briefing document. Besides, I wouldn't know what field strength to specify. Suggestions?

No material is radiation OR heat-proof, let alone both.
Good point. :P Do you like the "Earth's crust as a massive heat sink" better?

An apparently important contingency plan is EXPUNGED FROM ALL RECORDS?!?
Heh. "Access Denied" may be more appropriate.

I can't see this being contained at those levels by anything the Foundation could create.
Well despite its massive power output, this thing is pretty easily corralled and locked away. The problem is in getting rid of that waste heat … which I'm inviting suggestions for.

Absolutely. This needs to be fixed, narratively as well as astronomically.
Where do you have problems with the narrative? I'm open for suggestions to fix that, as well.

Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely. This needs to be fixed, narratively as well as astronomically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. (Ignoring the fact that this beyond a necro post, I agree none the less with the above statement)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was asked yesterday which SCP was my least favorite. This one came to my mind first. I hate this thing more every time I look at it.

"SCP-037 is magnetically contained at Site-32."
Magnetically containing things and not stating a field strength is becoming a pet-peeve of mine.

"It is housed in a small room composed of 1 m thick heat/radiation-proof polymer…"
No material is radiation OR heat-proof, let alone both.

"In the contingency of a [DATA EXPUNGED]."
An apparently important contingency plan is EXPUNGED FROM ALL RECORDS?!?

"…with a luminosity of about 1*10-12 times that of our sun…"
Luminosity is a measure of total power output of a star. The sun's luminosity is about 4*1026 watts. So this outputs about 4*1014 watts, which is equivalent to the power output of 500,000 commercial nuclear power reactors. I can't see this being contained at those levels by anything the Foundation could create.

"Object Class: Safe"
You state the containment walls need to be repaired, that the star is turning into a red giant, and that it will probably blow up some day. This thing is not safe.

This is definitely an interesting idea, but there is too much wrong with the details for me to like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the contingency of a [DATA EXPUNGED].

lolno

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Simple, short, interesting, and foreboding.

I don't quite know why it isn't Euclid though; if you lock it up and leave it, evidence seems to suggest it might do something catastrophic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would expect that to be a very important point, and consequently the sort of thing that would be mentioned / investigated / discussed in the reports…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Possibly. Once you open the skull, what you do is your own decision.

But hey, if you have to explain it :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Holbenilord is planning to eat ViperSRT3g's brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's sort of the point of the SCP. Every one of these objects is, in some way, impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something of this nature wouldn't be able to physically exist within this universe using our laws of physics.

So what you're saying is that it's an SCP? If so, well, that should be fairly obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know the post is old, but I believe Euclid should be the correct categorization. While it is not an immediate threat as of now, it could start the end of it's life-cycle at any time. If this were to happen, it would need immediate attention as to not destroy everything. The fact that it is not yet determined when it shall do so, or the required procedures to stop/reverse the events is what I think should make it Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Welcome to the SCP Foundation. I see you have need for some… mind-opening.

It's a pun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I am wondering is how would an object like this be able to exist? If it is determined to be a star that is undergoing nuclear fission, how can it do so if it's so tiny? The gravity created by something so small that can actually produce fission would be on the verge of becoming a black hole. (If Earth were compressed to the size of a marble, the atoms that comprise it would all be touching each other, and would collapse into a black hole). Something of this nature wouldn't be able to physically exist within this universe using our laws of physics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think Keter requires that it be directly dangerous to the world at large, in the immediate. The star is contained at present, is not malicious, and is only exceedingly dangerous at some later period of time.

I could be wrong, but I think it's fine at Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't this be Keter? I mean, it'll engulf the earth and destroy humanity if they don't figure out how to stop its growth…

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Much better, now that it's a real threat and the silliness is gone. +2 swing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: April fools idea: change "the Beaufort Sea etc." to "Bethlehem, Israel".

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
Awwww, it's cute! Still wouldn't want it anywhere near me unless it was behind a heat sink, possibly a steam plant to absorb the energy "radioisotope-generated electric power system", and a good meter or two of concrete.

Catch a falling star, indeed. Eep. +1

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I smoothed over the article again, perhaps this will entice a few more people to take a look. I'd like to get rid of the negative rating, if possible. If anyone sees something that could be improved please let me know!

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: I personally like the new edit. Very good work, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if this one will be able to survive even after my edits, but here you go. It's still a star, but now it is approaching the end of its life-cycle, which may prove disastrous for those of us here on earth. I toyed with the idea of having the foundation use it as a power source, since a star is by definition a fusion reactor, but I resisted the impulse as being too cute. We'll see how it goes.

item.title: Edit
item.content:encoded: I'm going to try an edit unless anyone objects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The date for editing this entry has elapsed. No edit has been attempted. This entry will be deleted Friday (10/2/09).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This entry has been reviewed and slated for editing. If you are the original author, please review this entry and comments, and see what changes could be made. Other users may edit ONLY:

  1. If the original author cannot be found/contacted
  2. If the original author gives permission

If no editing has been done on this entry after a period of two weeks, this entry will come under review for deletion. If you have any questions, please contact a mod or admin.

Start Date: 9/11/09
End Date: 9/25/09

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: It's a cool idea but it just doesn't show enough in the article. There needs to be more of a "this is a motherfucking star on earth!" idea rather than, "just a star, no big deal". I mean, I understand the foundation should be very objective but it should at least have some incident report or something to make it more interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm…

This has a fairly negative rating. While I enjoy this article, this may be because of nostalgia goggles, and the fact that it was one of the first original SCP's.

For those of you who truly think this is a bad article, please respond in why, and how you think it could be improved upon, or even if it could be at all.

item.title: A bit of a scale problem
item.content:encoded: If the dwarf star has one billionth the luminosity of the sun like the entry says, then unless I'm mistaken that's still 385 million billion watts. In other words about twenty-five thousand times the energy used by the entire human race. Or, if you're using long billions, only twenty-five times world energy use. The containment procedures don't seem to reflect this.
